topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on my screen, a jarring splash of unwelcome familiarity. 'Mark R.' The name hit me like a physical shove. My jaw tightened almost imperceptibly. I stared at the profile picture—a forced, overly confident grin that I remembered too well from the dusty hallways of middle school. A slow burn started low in my chest, a tight, unyielding knot that refused to loosen. I didn't tap the request. I didn't even open the message window. Instead, I minimized the app entirely, focusing instead on the intricate pattern of dust motes dancing in the afternoon sun slanting through my window. It felt like a challenge, a deliberate provocation, and I had no intention of yielding ground. I kept scrolling through mundane tasks, deliberately avoiding the digital space where this unwanted connection resided.

[story 2]

When the ping arrived, my stomach clenched. I picked up my phone, the glass cool against my palm, and saw the familiar name. A wave of hot, prickly annoyance washed over me. I could feel my shoulders rising toward my ears, a defensive posture taking hold. Why would he do this now? After all this time, after I’d built a wall around myself brick by painstaking brick? I let the notification sit there, mocking me. I didn't delete it, but I didn't accept it either. I went to the kitchen, needing the sharp, cold sensation of ice water on my wrists. Every muscle in my body felt coiled, ready to spring, refusing to relax into whatever this intrusion demanded. I resolved, with a stubborn set of my jaw, to ignore it completely, to let the digital ghost drift away.

[story 3]

The alert was undeniable. A familiar, unwelcome presence had reappeared in my feed. I felt a distinct hardening in my chest, a refusal to acknowledge the intrusion. I didn't rush to check the details; I paused, letting the silence stretch, amplifying the tension building inside me. My gaze drifted to the windowpane, tracing the condensation lines with my finger. It was a clear signal: *Do not engage.* I stood up, pacing the small room three times, the rhythm sharp and determined. A low, steady hum of internal resistance vibrated through me. Accepting this connection felt like conceding a battle I hadn't even bothered to fight properly. I made a conscious decision: this request would receive no attention whatsoever.
